coredata.dc:title: N, S co-doped carbon quantum dots/TiO2 composite for visible-light-driven photocatalytic reduction of Cr (VI)
coredata.dc:creator: Chang, Lantian
Ahmad, Noman
Zeng, Ganning
Ray, Ajay
Zhang, Yan
coredata.dc:description: A novel carbon quantum dots (CQDs) sensitized TiO2 (N, S-CQDs/TiO2) composite was prepared and used as a visible-light-active photocatalyst for the reduction of Cr (VI), one of the most toxic heavy metal ions. Experimental results from the batch reaction process indicated that the composite was able to completely reduce Cr (VI) to Cr (III) at pH 2.0 after 3 h′ exposure to visible light with the initial Cr (VI) concentration being 20.0 mg/L and a catalyst dosage of 1.0 g/L. It was also found that lower pH and lower initial concentration of Cr (VI) led to high reduction efficiency. Compare with pure TiO2, a four-fold increase in reduction efficiency of Cr (VI) was achieved by the N, S-CQDs/TiO2 composite under the same reaction conditions. The prepared composite also demonstrated good reusability and stable photocatalytic performance in real water samples. Results from this study confirm that sensitization of TiO2 with CQDs is efficient in broadening the light absorption range of TiO2, expanding its application in visible-light-driven photocatalysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Visible-light-driven photocatalysis
N, S-CQDs
TiO2
Cr (VI) reduction

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the N, S-CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites
Reduction of Cr (VI) under visible light irradiation
Characterization of N, S-CQDs and N, S-CQDs/TiO2
Photocatalytic study of N, S-CQDs/TiO2 composite
Effect of pH on the photocatalytic performance
Effect of the initial concentration of Cr (VI)
Reduction of Cr (VI) from real water samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Sciences and Engineering Research Council of Canada

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by NSERC Discovery Grant ( RGPIN-2017–04510 ) and Seed Fund from Memorial University .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HRTEM (a), XPS survey spectrum (b), high resolution XPS spectra of C 1s (c), O 1s(d), N1s (e), and S 2p (f), FT-IR spectrum (g), UV–vis absorption (h), and PL emission spectra (i) of N, S-CQDs.
Powder XRD (a), diffuse reflectance UV/Vis spectra (b), Tauc plot (c), and valence band XPS spectra of N, S-CQDs/TiO2.

Photocatalytic reduction of Cr (VI) over N, S-CQDs/TiO2 prepared from 4.0 g/L of N, S-CQDs solution) (a); efficiency of photoreduction of Cr (VI) by N, S-CQDs/TiO2 composites prepared from solutions with different concentrations of N, S-CQDs (1.0 −5.0 g/L).

Photocatalytic degradation of Cr (VI) at different pH values by N, S-CQDs/TiO2 under visible light irradiation (a), first-order reduction kinetics of Cr (VI) at different pH values (b). Reaction condition: Cr (VI) concentration = 20.0 mg/L, catalyst dosage = 1.0 g/L., *pH 5.8 is the natural pH value of the Cr (VI) aqueous solution.
Photocatalytic degradation of Cr (VI) over N, S-CQDs/TiO2 with different initial concentrations of Cr (VI).
Reusability of N, S-CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites toward degradation of Cr (VI) for four reaction cycles.
EIS Nyquist plots for pristine TiO2 and the N, S-CQDs/TiO2 composite (a), PL spectra of TiO2 and N, S-CQDs/TiO2 (b). The tested materials for PL spectroscopy are solid powders.
High-resolution XPS spectra and deconvoluted BE peaks of Ti 2p, and O 1 s before and after photocatalytic reduction of Cr (VI).
Photocatalytic degradation of Cr (VI) from surface water by N, S-CQDs/TiO2 with different initial concentrations of Cr (VI).
Reduction rate constants of Cr (VI) at different pH by N, S-CQDs/TiO2 under visible light irradiation.
Reduction rate constants of Cr (VI) with different initial concentrations of Cr (VI) by N, S-CQDs/TiO2 under visible light irradiation.

head.ce:title: N, S co-doped carbon quantum dots/TiO2 composite for visible-light-driven photocatalytic reduction of Cr (VI)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Process Engineering, Memorial University of Newfoundland, St. John’s, Canada

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Process Engineering, Memorial University of Newfoundland

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Process Engineering, Memorial University of Newfoundland, St. John’s, Canada

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Environment, Zhejiang University of Technology, Hangzhou, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Environment, Zhejiang University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Environment, Zhejiang University of Technology, Hangzhou, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical and Biochemical Engineering, Western University, London, Canada

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Biochemical Engineering, Western University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical and Biochemical Engineering, Western University, London, Canada

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel carbon quantum dots (CQDs) sensitized TiO2 (N, S-CQDs/TiO2) composite was prepared and used as a visible-light-active photocatalyst for the reduction of Cr (VI), one of the most toxic heavy metal ions. Experimental results from the batch reaction process indicated that the composite was able to completely reduce Cr (VI) to Cr (III) at pH 2.0 after 3 h′ exposure to visible light with the initial Cr (VI) concentration being 20.0 mg/L and a catalyst dosage of 1.0 g/L. It was also found that lower pH and lower initial concentration of Cr (VI) led to high reduction efficiency. Compare with pure TiO2, a four-fold increase in reduction efficiency of Cr (VI) was achieved by the N, S-CQDs/TiO2 composite under the same reaction conditions. The prepared composite also demonstrated good reusability and stable photocatalytic performance in real water samples. Results from this study confirm that sensitization of TiO2 with CQDs is efficient in broadening the light absorption range of TiO2, expanding its application in visible-light-driven photocatalysis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The pollution of heavy metals in water resources is a worldwide challenge due to the adverse health effects of heavy metals. Chromium is one of the toxic heavy metals, generally existing in hexavalent (Cr (VI)) and trivalent (Cr (III)) forms, of which Cr (VI) is highly toxic, carcinogenic, mutagenic and results in several health issues such as liver damage, pulmonary problems, vomiting, and diarrhea [1–3]. Nonetheless, Cr (III) is relatively inert and less toxic [4,5]. As a result, the reduction of Cr (VI) to Cr (III) is one of the most effective ways to eliminate hazardous Cr (VI) from wastewater.

Compared with conventional catalytic reduction of Cr (VI) using noble metal catalysts [6,7], photocatalytic reduction of Cr (VI) has attracted widespread attention and strong interest because of its high degradation rate and relatively low cost [8–10]. Various semiconductors both with a wide band gap (TiO2, ZnO, ZnS, CeO2, etc.) and narrow band gap (CdS, MnO2, α-Fe2O3, SnS2, etc.) have been developed [11–17]. Nonetheless, these photocatalysts suffer from the limitation of fast recombination of the electron−hole pairs, low solar energy utilization efficiency, and/or potential secondary pollution due to the leaching of heavy metal ions under an acidic environment. Therefore, developing non-toxic and efficient visible-light responsive photocatalysts is desirable.

Recently, carbonaceous nanomaterial (CNM) sensitized TiO2 has been developed as visible-light active photocatalysts in wastewater treatment. Combining non-toxic CNM with TiO2 can effectively reduce the band gap energy [18], broaden the light absorption range [19,20] and improve the separation efficiency of photogenerated charges of TiO2 [21,22], leading to enhanced photocatalytic performance. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are zero-dimensional (0D) CNMs with excellent optical and chemical properties [23]. CQDs are currently the most promising CNMs for applications in photocatalysis, electrocatalysis, bioimaging, and sensing applications due to their quantum confinement, edge effect, biocompatibility, low toxicity, and photostability [24–26]. Moreover, most CQDs contain hydroxyl and carboxylic acid moieties at their surfaces, resulting in excellent aqueous solubility and suitability for subsequent functionalization with various organic, polymeric, inorganic, or biological species [27]. Finally, by doping lightweight heteroatoms (B, N, P, and S), electric conductivity, and light absorption and emission range of CQDs can be fine-tuned [28–30]. As such, CQDs can serve as photosensitizers to extend the visible-light photocatalytic activity of TiO2.

To date, a variety of CQDs/TiO2 composites have been fabricated as visible-light responsive photocatalysts for Cr (VI) reduction [31–36]. Carbon dots-TiO2 nanosheets (CDs-TNs) were synthesized by the facile hydrothermal method and applied for photoreduction of Cr (VI) under simulated sunlight illumination. The CDs-TNs can be excited by a broad range of sunlight and exhibit higher photoreduction efficiency under simulated sunlight [32]. A carbon dot-sensitized Ti3+ self-doped TiO2 composite was constructed using a low-temperature (135 °C) hydrothermal method and was applied to simultaneously reduce Cr (VI) and degrade Rhodamine B (RhB) under visible light irradiation. The experimental results indicated that RhB (10 mg/L) and Cr (VI) (40 mg/L) solutions were degraded completely in 30 and 120 min respectively, with the catalyst loading of 1.0 g/L [33]. In related work, a CQDs and Ti3+ co-modified TiO2-graphene (CQDs-TiO2−x/rGO) nanocomposite was fabricated to degrade bisphenol A and reduce Cr (VI) synergistically. Due to the introduction of CQDs and rGO, the prepared composite exhibited extended visible light absorption and large BET surface area, leading to enhanced photocatalytic activities towards organic degradation and Cr (VI) reduction [35]. Although these studies have confirmed that CQDs/TiO2 composites demonstrate great potential as efficient photocatalysts driven by visible light to reduce Cr (VI), it is still worth investigating their reusability and photocatalytic reduction performance in real water samples.

In this study, a facile hydrothermal method was used to fabricate novel nitrogen and sulfur co-doped CQDs, which were then incorporated into TiO2 to form the N, S-CQDs/TiO2 composite. Different analyses were used to assess the morphology, chemical content, and optical properties of the prepared N, S-CQDs, and N, S-CQDs/TiO2. The photocatalytic activity of the as-prepared composite was tested by the reduction of Cr (VI) from water under visible light irradiation. The effects of initial substrate concentration, catalyst dosage, and solution pH were thoroughly examined. Based on the obtained experimental results, a plausible mechanism for the Cr (VI) reduction by N, S-CQDs/TiO2 was proposed. Results from this study contribute to the understanding of the favorable role of CQDs in enhancing the photocatalytic performance of TiO2.

The citric acid (99.9 %), thiourea (>99 %), sodium sulfite, commercial TiO2 (P25), sodium dichromate dihydrate, diphenylacarbazide (DPC), sulfuric acid, hydrochloride acid, and acetone were purchased from Fisher Scientific Canada. All the chemicals were used as received without further purification. Ultrapure deionized (DI) water was used in all experiments.

N, S-CQDs were synthesized via a modified one-pot hydrothermal method according to previous studies [37,38]. Specifically, 1.09 mmol (0.21 g) of citric acid and 3.0 mmol (0.23 g) of thiourea were dissolved in 40.0 mL of DI water by ultrasonication. The mixture was then transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 6 h. After cooling to room temperature, the products were centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 30 min to remove the large particles. The supernatant was withdrawn and filtered through 0.22 µm membrane filters, followed by a further purification using dialysis membrane (molecular cut of 500 Da) against DI water for 8 h. Finally, the solid N, S-CQDs product was collected after freeze-drying.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the N, S-CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: The N, S-CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites were prepared by mixing 0.2 g of P25 and 40.0 mL of N, S–CQDs solutions with five different concentrations (1.0–5.0 g/L) of solid N, S–CQDs. The pH of the solutions was adjusted to 3.5 using hydrochloric acid and then mixed with 20.0 mL of ethanol under vigorous stirring for 6 h. The solution mixtures were then transferred to 100 mL Teflon-sealed autoclaves and heated at 150 °C for 6 h. After the hydrothermal reaction, the N, S–CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites were collected after centrifugation of the product solutions, sample washing (twice) with DI water and ethanol, and air-drying at 70 °C for 24 h.
A schematic diagram for the preparation process of N, S-CQDs, and N, S-CQDs/TiO2 composite can be found in the Supplementary materials.

The morphology and composition of N, S-CQDs, and N, S-CQDs/TiO2 were characterized using high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, Titan 80–300, FEI, USA) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, PHI 5000 VPIII, ULVAC-PHI, Japan) as well as The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR, Tensor II, Bruker, Germany) spectroscopy over a range from 400 to 4000 cm−1. XRD was used to determine the crystallinity of the prepared photocatalyst. The UV-Vis absorption spectra of N, S-CQDs and N, S-CQDs/TiO2 were measured using a UV-3600 UV-Vis-Nir spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The valence-band position of the N, S-CQDs/TiO2 was evaluated by performing valence-band XPS. The Horiba Fluorolog-QM fluorometer (Horiba Canada) was used to measure the photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the prepared photocatalysts. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurement was conducted on a Zahner IM6 (Zahner, Germany) electrochemical workstation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reduction of Cr (VI) under visible light irradiation
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic performances of the as-prepared N, S-CQDs/TiO2 composites were investigated toward the reduction of Cr (VI) irradiated by visible light (300 W Xenon lamp with a 420-nm cutoff filter) at 30 °C. The temperature of the photocatalytic reaction was controlled by a circulating water system (FP-50L, Julabo, USA). For each run, 50.0 mg of the N, S-CQDs/TiO2 composite was mixed with 50.0 mL of Cr (VI) aqueous solution under constant stirring. After 1 h of dark adsorption/reaction, photocatalytic reduction of Cr (VI) was triggered by turning on the xenon lamp. At each sampling point, a 1.0 mL aliquot of the suspension was withdrawn from the reactor and filtered using a 0.22 µm membrane filter. The Cr (VI) concentration was determined using a Cari-100 UV–vis spectrophotometer. To verify the repeatability of the reduction experiments, three repeated experimental runs were carried out with 20.0 mg/L of initial concentration of Cr (VI), pH 3.0, and 1.0 g/L of catalyst dosage. The average experimental error was within ± 3.0 %, demonstrating a very good experimental repeatability.

The concentration of Cr (VI) was determined via a diphenylcarbazide (DPC) colorimetric method, and the detailed steps are as follows: 1.0 mL of the filtered sample was mixed with 4.0 mL of DI water. Then, 100.0 μL of 1 + 1 H2SO4 and 100 μL of 1 + 1 H3PO4 were added to the mixture and mixed completely. Next, 400.0 μL of freshly prepared DPC solution was added to the mixture and allowed to sit for 5–10 min for full-color development. Finally, the concentration of Cr (VI) was determined by monitoring the absorbance of the solution at 540 nm wavelength using DI water as the reference.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of N, S-CQDs and N, S-CQDs/TiO2
ce:section.ce:para: TEM micrographs provide information about the morphology of N, S-CQDs. TEM images of N, S-CQDs with different resolutions have been presented in Fig. 1a and Fig. S1, from which monodispersed quasi-spherical N, S-CQDs are observed with a narrow size distribution (4–7 nm). The powder XRD pattern of N, S-CQDs is demonstrated in Fig. S2. N, S-CQDs have a characteristic diffraction peak of amorphous carbon centered at 2θ of 24.2°, indicating that N, S-CQDs are composed of aromatic carbon sheets oriented in random directions [39]. XPS survey spectrum (Fig. 1b) of N, S-CQDs indicates that N, S-CQDs consist of elements of C, O, N, and S with the elemental composition being 54.23 % of C, 35.34 % of O, 6.77 % of N, and 3.66 % of S. High-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1s is illustrated in Fig. 1c, from which three deconvoluted peaks at 284.8, 286.35, and 288.81 eV, which are attributed to the sp2 C (C–C/CC) in graphite, the sp3 C (C–O, or C–N), and the oxidized C (CO) [39,40], are observed. Similarly, the N 1s spectrum (Fig. 1d) has been deconvoluted into three BE peaks centering at 400.64, 401.48, and 402.17 eV, which can be attributed to pyridinic-N, pyrrolic-N, and N–H, respectively. XPS spectrum of N 1 s for N, S-CQDs from this study is consistent with those reported from open literature [39,41,42]. The two peaks of the O 1s spectrum (Fig. 1e) at 531.61 and 533.02 eV are assigned to the CO, and C–O bonds respectively [38,39]. The S 2p spectrum (Fig. 1f) of N, S-CQDs can be fitted into four peaks: BE peaks at 163.32 and 163.34 eV are the S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 doublet for C–S–C covalent bond, whereas peaks at 164.33 and 168.67 eV are assigned to oxidized sulfur (SOx) groups [43]. XPS results indicate that heteroatom doping of CQDs by N and S was successful.

IR spectrum of N, S-CQDs can be found in Fig. 1g, from which broad peaks in 3500–3100 cm−1 are observed for O–H and N–H groups in the as-prepared N, S-CQDs. A peak at 3034 cm−1 is for C−H stretching vibrations. Characteristic peaks at 2058 and 1709 cm−1 are corresponding to stretching vibrations of NCS and CO bonds. Two peaks at 1658 and 1556 cm−1 are due to the stretching vibrations of amide CO and aromatic CC [44,45]. Band peaks at 1408 and 1234 cm−1 can be ascribed to the stretching vibrations of C–N, and C−O. Whereas, peaks at 1086 and 677 cm−1 are for stretching vibration of CS and bending vibration of C−S, respectively. The results show good agreement with the results found in the literature using similar sources of carbon and nitrogen [44,45].

The UV-Vis absorption spectrum of N, S-CQDs (Fig. 1h) indicates three absorption bands at 333 nm, 420 nm, and 600 nm which originate from the n to π * transition of CO, CN, and CS/SO bonds [46]. The UV–V is results prove the visible light absorption ability of N, S-CQDs. To study the optical properties of the synthesized N, S-CQDs, PL spectra (Fig. 1i) of N, S-CQDs have been measured under different excitation wavelengths. The N, S-CQDs exhibit excitation-dependent PL. The strongest emission wavelength of N, S-CQDs shifts from 445 to 525 nm when the excitation wavelength varies from 300 to 500 nm. N, S-CQDs show the highest emission intensity at 445 nm under the excitation wavelength of 400 nm. Although there is still no convincing PL mechanism for CQDs, many studies have revealed that the PL behavior of CQDs is highly associated with the amount of oxygen-containing moieties [47].

To characterize the prepared N, S-CQDs/TiO2 composite, IR and powder x-ray diffraction (XRD) analyses have been carried out. IR spectrum of N, S-CQDs/TiO2 in Fig. S3 proves the incorporation of N, S-CQDs by TiO2, indicated by the band peaks at 2926, 1647, 1416 cm−1 that are attributed to the stretching vibrations of C−H, CO, and C−N, as discussed earlier with Fig. 1g. In addition, absorption peaks associated with the bending vibration of C−H and the stretching vibration of Ti−O are also observed at 1377 and 1061 cm−1 from Fig. S3 [32]. Powder XRD patterns of TiO2 and the N, S-CQDs/TiO2 composite are illustrated in Fig. 2a. The N, S-CQDs/TiO2 composite shows a similar XRD pattern to that of TiO2, indicating that the phase structure of TiO2 did not vary after the deposition of N, S-CQDs. Because of the amorphous nature of the N, S-CQDs, peak heights of the composite were slightly lower than those of pure TiO2 stemming from the dilution by N, S-CQDs. Characteristic diffraction peaks of the N, S-CQDs/TiO2 composite were in good agreement with those of pure TiO2 by the exposure of 25.32°, 37.84°, 48.04°, 54.08°, 62.72°, 68.96°, 70.3°, and 75.2° peaks, corresponding to the reflections from (101), (004), (200), (105), (204), (116), (220), and (215) crystal facets [48]. Nonetheless, the XRD peaks of the composite are slightly shifted to smaller angles as shown in the inset of Fig. 2a, indicating chemical and/or physical connections between N, S-CQDs and TiO2 [49].

Ultraviolet-visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV-Vis DRS) has been employed to study the optical properties of TiO2 (P25) and N, S-CQDs/TiO2 composite. Fig. 2b illustrates the UV absorbance spectra of P25 and the N, S-CQDs/TiO2 composite. From Fig. 2b it is observed that TiO2 can only absorb light with a wavelength below 393 nm, whereas, the absorption edge of the N, S-CQDs/TiO2 has been extended from 393 nm to 426 nm. This indicates that photosensitizing TiO2 with N, S-CQDs helps to improve the visible-light harvesting efficiency. The bandgap energies (E g) of TiO2 and the N, S-CQDs/TiO2 composite have been estimated based on the Tauc plot (Fig. 2c), from which Eg can be determined using the following equations [50],
(1)
( α h ν )
2
= A
(
h ν −
E
g
)
(2)
h ν = h c / λ
where α is the optical absorption coefficient; A is a transition probability-dependent constant; c (3.0 × 108 m·s−1) is the speed of light; h (6.626 × 10−34 J·Hz−1) is the Plank's constant, and v is the radiation frequency. As shown in Fig. 2c, the E g value of the N, S-CQDs/TiO2 is 3.04 eV, which is lower than that (3.20 Ev) of the pure TiO2.

XPS valence band (VB) spectrum (Fig. 2d) of N, S-CQDs/TiO2 has been measured to determine the VB position (E VB) of the composite, the E VB value of N, S-CQDs/TiO2 is estimated to be 2.7 eV, almost identical to the E VB value of TiO2 [51]. The conduction band energy (E CB = −0.34 eV) of the N, S-CQDs/TiO2 composite can then be determined by Mulliken electronegativity theory using Eq. (3),
(3)
E
CB
=
E
VB
−
E
g

Results from Fig. 2c&d indicate that the N, S-CQDs/TiO2 composite has a narrower band gap than TiO2, and is thus capable of exciting the electron that participates in Cr (VI) reduction at a relatively lower energy level (irradiation by visible light rather than UV light).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic study of N, S-CQDs/TiO2 composite
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic performance of the N, S-CQDs/TiO2 composite was investigated under visible light irradiation (λex> 420 nm) for the reduction of Cr (VI). Fig. 3a compares the concentration changes of Cr (VI) with time in the presence of N, S-CQDs/TiO2, pure TiO2 (P25), and in the absence of photocatalysts. Without the use of any photocatalyst, the reduction of Cr (VI) was undetectable. In the presence of photocatalysts, roughly 3.0 % and 10.0 % of Cr (VI) were adsorbed under dark conditions by TiO2 and N, S-CQDs/TiO2 composite respectively. After 3 h of visible light irradiation, Cr (VI) was completely reduced to Cr (III) by N, S-CQDs/TiO2, whereas, less than 20.0 % of Cr (VI) was reduced to Cr (III) by pristine TiO2 due to its extremely low visible response. The improved photocatalytic reduction efficiency by N, S-CQDs/TiO2 was mainly due to the incorporation of N, S-CQDs in the TiO2 particles, which has improved the light-harvesting efficiency of visible light (400–600 nm) and hence enhanced photocatalytic capability.

The reduction efficiencies of Cr (VI) by N, S-CQDs/TiO2 composites prepared by impregnation of TiO2 into solutions with different concentrations of N, S-CQDs (1.0 −5.0 g/L) are illustrated in Fig. 3b. Results indicated that the amounts of N, S-CQDs embedded on TiO2 increase with the increasing concentration of N, S-CQDs solutions. Nonetheless, there is no significant increase of the embedded N, S-CQDs when solution concentration is equal to or higher than 4.0 g/L due to the adsorption equilibrium. As such, the N, S-CQDs/TiO2 composite prepared by impregnating TiO2 with 4.0 g/L of N, S-CQDs solution was used for all the subsequent experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH on the photocatalytic performance
ce:section.ce:para: The speciation and mobility of chromium species in aqueous systems are governed by the pH and the presence of redox agents in an aqueous system [7,50]. Based on the Purboix Curve of Cr (VI), the significant species of Cr (VI) in an aqueous solution are H2CrO4 (aq.) and HCrO4 − below pH 6.5, and CrO4 2− above pH 6.5 [9,52]. To investigate the pH effect on the photocatalytic performance of the prepared N, S-CQDs/TiO2 composite, reduction of Cr (VI) was carried out under different pH (2.0, 3.0, 4.0, and 5.8 *) of initial Cr (VI) solutions.

As seen in Fig. 4a, the N, S-CQDs/TiO2 composite can reduce Cr (VI) more efficiently under acidic conditions. With the increase of solution pH, the Cr (VI) reduction rate decreases from 99.88 % at pH 2.0 to 75.70 % at pH 3.0, 62.90 % at pH 4.0% and 46.18 % at pH 5.8. This is because the surface of N, S-CQDs/TiO2 is negatively charged at a solution pH higher than pHPZC (the point of zero charge), which leads to the increased electrostatic repulsion between the catalyst and Cr (VI) species. Consequently, the migration of Cr (VI) species to the surface of the catalyst is inhibited, resulting in the decreased Cr (VI) reduction.

The influence of solution pH on the reduction kinetics was determined (Fig. 4b) using the first-order kinetic equation (Eq. (4)) and the derived reduction rate constants were summarized in Table 1. Results from Fig. 4b and Table 1 reveal that the reduction rate constants also decrease with the increasing solution pH.
(4)
ln
(
c
t
/
c
0
)
= − k t
where k is the apparent reduction rate constant, and t is the time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of the initial concentration of Cr (VI)
ce:section.ce:para: Effects of the initial concentration of Cr (VI) on the photocatalytic activity of the N, S-CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposite are also studied with the results being illustrated in Fig. 5a. With the same catalyst dosage, reaction temperature, and solution pH, the reduction efficiency of Cr (VI) decreases with the increasing initial concentration of Cr (VI). Since there is a maximum of active sites on the surface of the used N, S-CQDs/TiO2 composite (50.0 mg), which corresponds to a specific reduction capacity of Cr (VI). When the amount of Cr (VI) exceeds the reduction capacity of the composite (the case with c 0 = 40.0 g/L), a complete reduction of Cr (VI) is hardly achieved. Moreover, a solution with a high concentration of Cr (VI) absorbs more incident visible light, lowering the photon flux received by N, S-CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposite and the penetration of incident visible light into Cr (VI) aqueous solution [53].
The first-order reduction rate constants of Cr (VI) with different initial Cr (VI) concentrations were determined by linear regression (Fig. 5b) and the derived rate constants were listed in Table 2.

To further verify the chemical stability and reusability of the prepared N, S-CQDs/TiO2 composite, cyclic tests of photocatalytic reduction of Cr (VI) were carried out. After each photocatalytic reaction, the N, S-CQDs/TiO2 powders were separated from the solution by centrifugation (4000 rpm for 25 min) and washed with DI water. After drying at 70 °C in an oven, the N, S-CQDs/TiO2 composite was used for the next cycle of the photoreduction experiment. The reusability studies for N, S-CQDs/TiO2 in four reaction cycles are shown in Fig. 6. After two cycles, no detectable loss of photoactivity is observed, however, the photoreduction efficiency decreases by 33.1 % after the fourth cycle. It may be due to the detachment of the N, S-CQDs from TiO2 after repeated use as N, S-CQDs are highly soluble in water and can be released as leachate.

Based on the obtained experimental results, it is very clear that incorporating N, S-CQDs into TiO2 leads to enhanced photocatalytic reduction of Cr (VI) in the aqueous medium. Such performance enhancement is attributed to the extended light absorption range and the reduced band gap energy of the composite. As seen from Fig. 1g & 2b, incorporating π-conjugated N, S-CQDs into TiO2 significantly extend the light absorption edge and thus improve the visible light harvesting. The electronic coupling of the π states of N, S-CQDs and the conduction band (CB) of TiO2 induces a narrower nano-hybrid band gap compared to pure TiO2 and brings about a reduced band gap energy [18].

The EIS analysis has been performed to know the charge transfer kinetics for the Cr (VI) reduction by the N, S-CQDs/TiO2. The EIS Nyquist plots of TiO2 and N, S-CQDs/TiO2 are shown in Fig. 7a. Compared with TiO2, a much smaller arc radius is observed for N, S-CQDs/TiO2, which implies a faster charge transfer kinetics at the electrode/electrolyte interface. Sensitization of TiO2 with N, S-CQDs significantly reduces the charge transfer resistance (R ct) and enhances the photocatalytic activity, as we know that catalytic activity is inversely proportional to the R ct [54]. To further understand the recombination and charge transfer efficiency of the prepared composite, PL spectra of TiO2 and the composite have been examined under the excitation wavelength of 300 nm. As seen in Fig. 7b, the N, S-CQDs/TiO2 has a much lower PL intensity than pristine TiO2. Materials with low intensity in PL spectra have a low recombination rate for the photogenerated charge carriers [34,55]. Results from Fig. 7b display that N, S-CQDs in the composite help to capture the photogenerated charge carriers and hence improve the carrier separation by retarding the recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes, bringing about an enhanced photocatalytic activity.

Based on the widely reported working mechanism of semiconductor photocatalysts, a possible pathway for the reduction of Cr (VI) by N, S-CQDs/TiO2 composite under visible light irradiation was speculated. When the N, S-CQDs/TiO2 composite is exposed to visible light (λ ≥ 420 nm), the photon energy absorbed by the composite stimulates the generation of electrons (e-) from the VB of the composite, and the photo-excited electrons are then quickly transferred from VB to the CB of the composite, leaving holes (h+) in the VB of the composite. Holes in the VB are involved in the oxidation of H2O to O2, accompanied by the release of reactive protons (H+). The protons and the excited electrons collectively reduce the toxic Cr (VI) species (mainly HCrO4 - and Cr2O7 2- over pH 2.0–6.0) to less toxic Cr (III) at the CB of the composite. The photocatalytic reduction of Cr (VI) by N, S-CQDs/TiO2 can be described by the following reactions.
(5)
N , S − C Q D s / T i
O
2
+ h ν →
e
C B
−
+
h
V B
+
(6)
2
H 2
O + 4

O 2
(7)

4
−
+ 3

−
+ 7
H

→ C
r

+ 4
H
2

(8)

2
O
7
2 −
+ 6
e
−
+ 14
H
+
→ 2 C
r
3 +
+ 7
H
2
O

To verify the speculated working mechanism of Cr (VI) reduction by N, S-CQDs/TiO2, high-resolution XPS spectra of Ti 2p, O 1s, and Cr 2p of the composite before and after Cr (VI) reduction were measured. Fig. 8a shows the XPS spectrum of Ti 2p in the fresh composite, which has the doublet at 458.65 and 464.41 eV, responsible for Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2, correspondingly [56]. After Cr (VI) reduction, the binding energy (BE) peaks of Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2 shift toward higher binding energy (Figs. 8b) to 458.75 and 464.55 eV, revealing that Ti donates electrons during the photocatalytic reduction process. In the O 1s XPS spectrum of the fresh catalyst (Fig. 8c), two deconvoluted BE peaks at 529.83 and 531.11 eV are attributed to the Ti–O, and C–O of the Ti-O-C bond formed in the prepared composite. After Cr (VI) reduction, the corresponding peaks all shift slightly to higher BE positions at 529.93 and 531.12 eV (Fig. 8d) [57,58]. No significant BE peaks of Cr 2p were observed from N, S-CQDs/TiO2 before and after Cr (VI) reduction, indicating that the reduction of Cr (VI) was not accompanied by the formation of Cr(OH)3 (precipitate) significantly. XPS results prove that the photo-generated electrons contribute to Cr (VI) reduction under visible light irradiation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reduction of Cr (VI) from real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: To further test the stability and photocatalytic activity of the as-prepared N, S-CQDs/TiO2 composite, the reduction of Cr (VI) in real water samples was conducted under the same reaction conditions as those in DI water. The surface water collected from Robin Hood Bay Landfill (NL, Canada) was selected to replace DI water for the photocatalytic reduction of Cr (VI). Fig. 9 shows the reduction kinetics of Cr (VI) from surface water with two different initial concentrations (20.0 and 40.0 mg/L, pH 2.0). It can be seen from Fig. 9 that the 98.0 % and 89.0 % of Cr (VI) have been removed and reduced by N, S-CQDs/TiO2 from surface water within 3 h with initial concentrations of Cr (VI) being 20.0 and 40.0 mg/L, respectively. The reduction rate constants are 0.0283 and 0.0125 min−1 for the two cases as illustrated in the inset of Fig. 9. These results reveal that the prepared N, S-CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposite can effectively reduce Cr (VI) from real water samples under visible light irradiation.

In this study, the N, S-CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposite was prepared and employed as a visible-light-responsive photocatalyst to reduce Cr (VI) under acidic conditions. Characterization by UV-Vis DRS, EIS, and PL spectroscopy indicated the attainment of an extended light absorption range, a narrower band gap, fast charge transfer, and less recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes by the prepared composite. The enhanced photocatalytic activity of the N, S-CQDs/TiO2 composite was confirmed by the batch photocatalytic reduction process of Cr (VI), from which a four-fold increase in reduction efficiency of Cr (VI) was achieved compared with pristine TiO2. Experimental results reveal that the N, S-CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposite was able to completely reduce Cr (VI) (20.0 mg/L) to Cr (III) at pH 2.0 under visible light (λ ≥ 420 nm) irradiation.

Lantian Chang: Investigation, Writing - original draft. Noman Ahmad: Investigation. Ganning Zeng: Writing - review & editing. Ajay Ray: Supervision. Yan Zhang: Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing – review and editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by NSERC Discovery Grant (RGPIN-2017–04510) and Seed Fund from Memorial University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jece.2022.108742.

ce:section.ce:para:
